segments.s411-g57: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. گشتاسپ شه is a name plus title (Gushtasp, the king) in apposition; the verb نیست 'is not / there is not' is used existentially (historical present here) and takes یک نامدار 'a single renowned one' as its indefinite object after the verb. The clause therefore says that at the time Gushtasp was king, there was none renowned.
segments.s411-g58: به is repeated as a prepositional/adverbial marker meaning 'in/at' or 'as to' and introduces each sphere: رزم (battle), بزم (banquet/revelry), رای (counsel/plan), and شکار (hunt). The repeated structure 'به X و به Y و به Z و W' lists the domains in which no one was famed.
segments.s411-g59: پذیرفت is a past transitive verb 'he accepted/embraced'. The noun phrase پاکیزه دین بهی functions as its object: پاکیزه is an adjective 'pure/clean', دین is 'religion', and بهی here is an epithet 'the Good' (i.e. the Good Religion — a conventional phrase referring to the true faith).
segments.s411-g60: نهان گشت is a two-word predicate: نهان 'hidden' followed by the past intransitive گشت 'became' meaning 'became hidden' or 'were concealed'. گمراهی و بی‌رهی are coordinated nouns 'going astray / misguidance' and 'lack of guidance / straying' — the things that were hidden (i.e. removed).
segments.s411-g61: چو 'when' introduces a temporal clause. خورشید 'sun' is used metaphorically for a luminous leader; شد 'became' is the past intransitive. راه گیهان is a genitive phrase 'the way/guide of the world' (راه modified by گیهان), and خدیو follows as an appositional/title meaning 'ruler/sovereign' (identifying the one who is the world's guide).
segments.s411-g62: نهان شد is again 'became hidden' (نهان before the verb, with شد as auxiliary/past 'became'). بدآموزی 'bad instruction / corrupting influence' and راه دیو 'the demon's way' are the coordinated items that were hidden/removed — i.e., evil teaching and the devilish path were driven away.
segments.s411-g63: ازان پس 'after that' gives the temporal frame; که introduces the subordinate temporal clause 'when'. ارجاسپ آمد به جنگ is a straightforward clause: ارجاسپ (proper name) + آمد 'came' + به جنگ 'to battle' (directional phrase).
segments.s411-g64: سپه is the subject 'army/host'. چون introduces a simile 'like/as'; پلنگان 'leopards' is the image applied to the host. The second coordinated part 'و مهتر نهنگ' parallels that: مهتر 'chief/leader' is likened to نهنگ 'whale' (a metaphor for great size/power). Word order places the simulated images after the nouns they qualify.
segments.s411-g41: که introduces a relative/nominal clause ('that' / 'which'). چندین precedes the noun as an indefinite quantifier 'many/several'; بزرگی is an abstract noun here meaning 'honours/grandeur'. The coordinating و links the subsequent nouns گنج (treasure) and سپاه (army), so the whole segment lists things obtained.
segments.s411-g42: گرانمایه is an adjectival modifier 'precious/valuable' that scopes over the following nouns. اسپان (plural of horse) names prized horses; the following و links تخت (throne) and کلاه (head-gear, here 'crown/helmet'). The short, paratactic list continues the catalogue begun in the previous segment.
segments.s411-g43: ز (poetic form of از) marks origin/source: 'from'. پیش here functions with ز to mean 'from before/of old' and together with نیاکان 'from the ancestors'. ما is the possessor 'our'. یافتی is 2nd-person past 'you obtained/received' and is the main verb of this clause.
segments.s411-g44: چو (= وقتی/when) introduces a temporal clause. در + بندگی = 'in servitude/into service'; تیز is an adverbial adjective here 'swift/quick'. بشتافتی is the 2nd-person past/preterite 'you hastened/rushed', so the clause means 'when you hastened into servitude'.
segments.s411-g45: چه (interrogative/exclamatory) = 'what/how much'. مایه here means 'resource/wealth'. جهان follows as the domain 'in the world'. داشت is the simple past 'had'. لهراسپ شاه is the noun phrase 'Lohrasp the king' in apposition to the possession described.
segments.s411-g46: نکردی is the negative past 2nd-person singular 'you did not (do)'. گذر here is a noun 'a visit/passage' and is the object of نکردی; سوی آن بارگاه means 'toward that palace/court'. Together: 'you did not make a visit to that court.'
segments.s411-g47: چو = 'when'. او = 'he'. شهر ایران = 'the city/land of Iran' (direct object). به + (proper name) marks the recipient ('to/for Gushtasp'), and داد is the past 'gave'. The clause states a past transfer: 'when he gave Iran to Gushtasp.'
segments.s411-g48: نیامد is negative past 'did not come' (an impersonal construction here: 'no memory came'). ت is the 2nd-person clitic (t) attaching to indicate 'to you' and را is the object/contrast marker (together giving ت را = 'to you'). هیچ = 'not a single/none', زان = 'of/from that', تخت یاد = 'memory/trace of the throne'. The whole means 'not a single memory of that throne came to you.'
segments.s411-g17: بگویش is the imperative bēgū + enclitic -esh 'tell him' (verb before clause). که begins a reported/subordinate clause 'that'. هرکس ... که is a correlative relative 'whoever ... who/that'; گردد is the present/subjunctive/optative of گشتن 'become' used here as 'becomes', and بلند is an adjectival predicate 'lofty/raised' modifying the implied role (i.e. high/lofty in rank).
segments.s411-g18: This continues the subordinate clause: جهاندار is the nominal result 'world‑ruler/sovereign'. وز here is an archaic fused preposition/particle (functioning like از/و 'and/from') linking the next phrase; هر بدی 'every evil' (هر = every) and بی‌گزند 'unharmed' is a predicate/adverbial phrase meaning 'free from harm'. The phrase as a whole says that whoever becomes a lofty sovereign is safe from all evil.
segments.s411-g19: ز (poetic 'from/to') marks the source/recipient 'to the Creator'. باید is the impersonal necessity 'it is necessary/one must'. که introduces the subordinate clause 'that he has/holds ...'; دارد here is the present tense 'has/keeps', and سپاس is the direct object 'thanks/praise' — together: 'one must have/offer thanks to the Creator.'
segments.s411-g20: که is the relative/conjunctive 'for/that'. اویست is a poetic form combining او + است (copula) meaning 'he is' (here used as a copula; annotated senses treat it as a proper name, but context functions as 'he is'). جاوید is an adjective 'eternal', and نیکی شناس is a nominal compound 'knower of goodness' (نیکی = goodness, شناس = knower).
segments.s411-g21: چو باشد forms a temporal/conditional clause 'when/if there is' (چو = when/if, باشد = subjunctive/present of 'be'). فزاینده is a participial adjective 'increasing', modifying نیکویی 'goodness/virtue' — 'when increasing goodness exists' (i.e. 'when goodness increases').
segments.s411-g22: به پرهیز دارد is idiomatic: به + پرهیز 'by/with restraint' and دارد 'he has/keeps' (دارد + direct object). سر از ... داشتن is a figurative expression 'to keep one's head away from ...' so سر (head) + از (from) + بدخویی (ill‑temper/bad behavior) yields 'he keeps his head away from ill‑temper' — i.e. he refrains from bad temper by restraint.
segments.s411-g23: بیفزایدش is the verb بیفزاید 'adds/increases' with the enclitic -ش ('him' or 'it' as object): 'it increases/adds to him'. کامگاری و گنج are the coordinated objects 'success (fulfillment) and treasure' — verb + object order is verbal and the enclitic marks the beneficiary.
segments.s411-g24: بود is the past/attributive copula 'was/was there' and predicates شادمان 'joyful' (adjective). در introduces the locative phrase 'in', and سرای سپنج is the place 'the house/abode of Sepanj' (سپنج here is a proper/place name). The clause states he was joyful in that house.
segments.s411-g129: بباشیم is a hortative/subjunctive first-person-plural form meaning “let us be/go” or “let us do X.” پیشش is the prepositional phrase ‘in his presence/before him’ (پیش + clitic ش). بخواهش is the verb بخواه (ask/plead) with the object clitic -ش ‘him’; it follows the locative phrase and takes به + پای (‘at the feet’) as the manner/place of pleading. Word order is typical Classical Persian: hortative verb + prepositional phrase + verb with object clitic + prepositional phrase of manner.
segments.s411-g130: ز (poetic) marks origin/cause: ‘from.’ The repetition ز خشم و ز کین provides parallelism ‘from anger and from hatred.’ آرمش is a noun ‘calm/peace’; باز modifies the action as ‘again’; جای is ‘place/position’ (here: restore to his former place). Together the phrase expresses the aim/result: that, out of anger and hatred, he be returned/returned to peace and to his place again.
segments.s411-g131: نمانم is the first-person singular present-subjunctive/optative with negative sense ‘I will not remain / I will not wait / I will not stand by.’ که introduces the subordinate clause describing what the speaker will not permit. بادی is a nominal form meaning ‘a breeze/a gust (of wind),’ بتو = به + تو ‘to/at you,’ and بر وزد is a third-person singular present-subjunctive of وزیدن ‘to blow’ with the prefix بر indicating direction ‘upon/over.’ The whole clause says the speaker will not stay while even a breeze blows upon you.
segments.s411-g132: بران سان is an adverbial phrase ‘in/with that manner’ or ‘in such a way’; که is the relative/conjunction ‘that’ introducing the effect. از گوهر من literally ‘from my essence’ = ‘to my being/nature.’ سزد is an impersonal/present form (‘it befits / it is fitting’). Together the clause means ‘in such a way that it would be fitting to my very being,’ i.e. something the speaker could not tolerate.
segments.s411-g33: همان بر ... همان بدروی is a compact, proverb-like clause: بر marks the target/direction ('toward, upon'), که introduces the action you perform, and the two همان ('the same') frame the symmetry. کاری here is a nominal/elliptical reference to 'the deed you do' (poetic economy) and بدروی is an archaic 2nd-person outcome-form meaning 'you will receive/meet with' (i.e. 'it will be done to you'). The whole segment expresses 'as you act toward (others), so the same will be done to you.'
segments.s411-g34: سخن هرچه گویی همان بشنوی is a parallel clause: سخن is the topic 'speech/words', هرچه گویی is a relative/conditional phrase 'whatever you say' with گویی functioning as a present/optative 2nd-person verbal form, and همان بشنوی places the emphasising pronoun 'the same' before the verb بشنوی ('you will hear' / 'you hear the same'). Word order is topic + relative clause + emphatic object + verb.
segments.s411-g35: کنون از تو اندازه گیریم راست: کنون ('now') sets the time, از تو marks the person being measured, and اندازه گیریم is a verbal phrase with گیریم (1st-person plural present/subjunctive/jussive) meaning 'we take/measure'. راست functions adverbially ('fairly/justly' or 'it is right'). The clause means 'now we measure you rightly.'
segments.s411-g36: نباید بر این بر فزون و نه کاست is an impersonal prohibition: نباید ('one should not / it must not be') + بر این ('on this') introduces the object, then the correlative negative pair بر فزون ... نه کاست = 'to make (it) more ... nor to make (it) less.' The construction نه ... و نه ... (here 'و' linking the two verbs) yields 'neither ... nor ...'; فزون is used adjectivally/adverbially as 'more/increased' and کاست is an older verbal/participial form meaning 'to decrease / to take away.'
segments.s411-g37: که بگذاشتی سالیان بی‌شمار: که introduces an explanatory clause ('for/that'), بگذاشتی is a past/perfect 2nd-person form of گذاشتن used here to mean 'you spent/you passed' (poetic perfect), سالیان is the plural object 'years', and بی‌شمار is a postpositive adjective 'countless.' The phrase means 'that you passed countless years.'
segments.s411-g38: به گیتی بدیدی بسی شهریار: به گیتی is a locative phrase 'in the world/earth', بدیدی is the past 2nd-person form 'you saw', and بسی شهریار places the quantifier بسی ('much/many/plenty') before شهریار ('king, lord'). The clause states 'you saw many a king in the world.'
segments.s411-g39: اگر بازجویی ز راه خرد: اگر introduces the conditional 'if', بازجویی is a verbal noun meaning 'inquiry/questioning', and ز راه خرد is a prepositional phrase 'by/from the way of wisdom' (ز = by/from, راه خرد = 'the path of wisdom'). Together: 'If you inquire by the way of wisdom...'.
segments.s411-g40: بدانی که چونین نه اندر خورد: بدانی is a 2nd-person present/subjunctive form used here with future/epistemic force 'you will know' or 'you would know'; که introduces the content-clause 'that', چونین = 'such/thus', and نه اندر خورد is an idiomatic negated verb phrase where اندر خورد (literally 'enter/eat') means 'to befit / to be fitting' or 'to apply' — negated by نه. So: 'you will know that such a thing does not fit / is not appropriate.'
segments.s411-g65: This clause uses the negative past verb ندانست (ن‑ + دانست) meaning 'did not know.' کس here functions as an indefinite negative 'no one' (classical usage: 'ندانست کس' = 'no one knew'). لشکرش is the direct object with the possessive clitic -ش ('his army/host'), and را is the Persian definite/object marker attached to لشکرش. شمار is a noun 'number/count' that is the semantic object of دانست.
segments.s411-g66: پذیره here functions as 'reception'/'welcome' (a noun/past participial sense). شدش is archaic: شد + enclitic ش referring to 'him' and in this construction has the force 'befell him' / 'was given to him' (so: 'he was received' / 'he had a reception'). نامور شهریار is an appositive/nominal phrase 'the famed king' linked to that event; word order is inverted (verb/phrase before the title), a typical epic/poetic ordering.
segments.s411-g67: یکی here is an indefinite 'one / someone / he' used in narrative to introduce an action ('he made'). گورستان is the noun 'cemetery' and کرد is the simple past 'did/made' (کردن). بر is the preposition 'on/upon,' دشت is 'plain,' and کین qualifies the plain as 'of vengeance' (genitive relationship expressed by juxtaposition).
segments.s411-g68: که introduces a result/relative clause ('so that/whereby'). پیدا here means 'visible/apparent.' نبد is the archaic negative past/present form meaning 'was not / did not exist' (negation of بودن/شدن). پهن modifies روی as 'broad/wide,' روی means 'surface' (here 'surface of the earth'), and زمین is 'earth/ground.' The whole clause expresses result: the broad surface of the earth was not to be seen.
segments.s411-g69: همانا is an emphatic interjection 'verily/truly.' که تا is a two-part connector: که here begins the clause and تا means 'until.' رستخیز is 'Resurrection' (or the great rising), and این سخن is 'this speech/this tale' (the demonstrative plus noun). Together they set the temporal limit 'until the Resurrection.'
segments.s411-g70: میان بزرگان means 'among the great/nobles' (میان = among; بزرگان = great ones). نگردد is the negative present/subjunctive form of گشتن/گردیدن used here in the sense 'will not become / will not turn into' (archaic inflection). کهن is the predicate adjective 'old/ancient,' so the clause states that the tale 'shall not become old among the great.'
segments.s411-g71: کنون is 'now.' خاور او is 'his east' (خاور = east; او = his), with a possessive relation. راست here means 'straight/direct/continuous' (adverbial sense 'straightway' or 'directly'), and تا introduces a range 'as far as/unto.' باختر is 'the west.' The phrase sets a span 'from his east straight to the west.'
segments.s411-g72: همی is an archaic/adverbial particle here meaning 'continually/indeed.' بشکند is the present/subjunctive-like form of شکستَن used in narrative as a simple present 'breaks' or 'shatters' (here 'breaks the back of'). پشت is 'back' (body/behind), شیران is plural of شیر 'lions' (often used literally or metaphorically for brave warriors), and نر specifies 'male' (so 'male lions'). The verb agrees with the ongoing violent action described.
segments.s411-g25: چو is the conditional/time conjunction 'when/if'. دوری گزیند is a verb phrase where دوری (avoidance / keeping away) is the object and گزیند (third-person present of گزیدن) means 'chooses' or 'keeps'; the construction means 'chooses avoidance' = 'keeps away'. ز is the poetic form of از 'from' and links to کردار زشت 'from/away from evil deeds'. Word order is typical poetic Persian: conjunction + noun + verb + preposition + noun phrase.
segments.s411-g26: بیابد is a third‑person present ('finds' / 'will find') of یافتن, giving the resultant clause. بدان here corresponds to به + آن 'to/ in that', introducing گیتی 'world' (بدان گیتی = 'in/that world'); اندر is another locative 'in/within', and بهشت is 'paradise'. The clause literal order (verb → locative phrase → goal) is poetic but straightforward: 'he finds (بیابد) in that world (بدان گیتی) a paradise (اندر بهشت)'.
segments.s411-g27: بد و نیک 'bad and good' places the two opposites in parallel. بر ما همی بگذرد uses بر 'upon/on' + indirect object ما 'us' with همی (an emphatic/continuous adverb) and بگذرد (third‑person present of گذشتن) meaning 'passes' or 'befalls'—so: 'bad and good pass upon us' / 'we experience both good and bad.'
segments.s411-g28: چنین داند 'knows this' places چنين (thus/this) before the verb داند (knows). آن کس که دارد خرد is a relative clause identifying the knower: آن کس 'that person' + که دارد خرد 'who has wisdom' (دارد = has, خرد = wisdom). The overall order is slightly inverted for emphasis: 'Thus knows that one who has wisdom' → 'Whoever has wisdom knows this.'
segments.s411-g29: سرانجام is an adverb meaning 'at last' or 'in the end'. بستر بود 'the bed was' uses بستر (bed) as subject and بود (past of بودن) as copula. تیره‌خاک is a compound noun 'dark earth' (i.e. the grave/soil) and functions as the predicate: 'the bed was dark earth' (poetic for 'in the end our bed is the grave').
segments.s411-g30: بپرد is a present/subjunctive‑flavored third‑person verb from پریدن 'to fly/launch', here 'sets forth' or 'takes flight'. روان is the soul/spirit as grammatical subject following the verb (poetic verb–subject order). سوی یزدان پاک is a directional phrase 'toward the pure God' (سوی = toward, یزدان = God, پاک = pure). Together: 'the soul flies toward the pure God.'
segments.s411-g31: به گیتی 'in the world' (به = in/for, گیتی = world) sets the domain. هرانکس که نیکی شناخت is a relative clause: هرانکس 'whoever' + که 'who' + نیکی شناخت literally 'has recognized goodness' (شناخت here expresses recognition/knowledge of goodness). Word order places the domain first, then the identifying relative clause.
segments.s411-g32: بکوشید is an imperative/optative or hortatory present of کوشیدن 'strive/try' ('should strive' / 'strive'). و = 'and' links the two verbs. با شهریاران 'with the rulers' uses با 'with' + شهریاران (plural of شهریار 'king/ruler'). بساخت is a poetic/archaic verbal form of ساختن 'to make/arrange' (here meaning 'make allies, arrange relations, reconcile' with rulers) and follows the exhortative sense started by بکوشید: 'strive and make (ties) with rulers.'
segments.s411-g1: بفرمود is an archaic/honorific past verb meaning 'he commanded/said'. تا here is the purposive subordinating particle 'so that / in order that' introducing the following clause. بهمن is the personal name (the one ordered), آمدش is آمد + enclitic -ش 'to him' meaning 'came to him', and پیش is an adverb 'before / in the presence of'; word order is Verb (بفرمود) + subordinator (تا) + noun (بهمن) + verb+clitic (آمدش) + adverb (پیش).
segments.s411-g2: ورا is the prepositional pronoun 'to him'; پندها is the plural noun 'admonitions / pieces of advice' and داد is the past verb 'gave'. ز اندازه بیش is an idiomatic quantifier 'beyond measure' where ز is the poetic 'from/of', اندازه 'measure/degree', and بیش 'more/excess'; this whole phrase modifies the manner/extent of the giving.
segments.s411-g3: بدو گفت literally 'he said to him' (بدو = 'to him', گفت = 'said' in classical usage). اسپ سیه is the noun phrase 'black steed/horse'. بر نشین is an imperative construction: بر + نشین 'sit upon / mount' (literally 'sit on') directed at the addressee; the imperatives are reported after گفت.
segments.s411-g4: بیارای is an archaic imperative meaning 'adorn (yourself)' here commanding adornment; تن را is the definite object 'the body' with the object marker را. به + دیبای چین expresses the instrument/cloth used: به ... دیبای چین = 'in/with Chinese brocade' (چین = China, forming the adjective). Word order is imperative verb + object + prepositional phrase indicating material.
segments.s411-g5: بنه is an archaic imperative 'put / place' (imperative of an older verbal root), followed by بر سرت 'on your head' (بر = on; سرت = سر + enclitic ت 'your head'). افسر خسروی is the noun phrase 'kingly/royal helmet' (افسر = helmet, خسروی = royal). The order is imperative verb + locative phrase + noun phrase indicating the object to be placed.
segments.s411-g6: نگارش here functions substantively as 'its ornamentation / decoration' (an abstract noun), followed by همه گوهر پهلوی 'all the Pahlavi jewels' which specifies the decoration. همه 'all' quantifies گوهر 'gems/jewels', and پهلوی indicates the Pahlavi/Parthian quality or provenance; the phrase as a whole describes what the helmet is set with.
segments.s411-g7: بر آن سان که is an idiomatic manner phrase 'in that manner / so that' (بر + آن سان = 'in that way'), and introduces a result/consequence clause. هرکس که بیند ترا is a relative clause 'whoever sees you' (هرکس = whoever; که = relative conjunction; بیند = classical present/subjunctive 'sees' or 'may see'; ت = object suffix 'you'; را is the object marker).
segments.s411-g8: ز introduces the source/set 'from / among' (poetic use), گردنکشان is a collective noun 'the rebels' or 'neck‑breakers' (here 'rebels'), and برگزیند is a present/future/selecting verb 'will choose / will single out' with the enclitic ت 'you' as its object and را again marking the object (emphatic/definite). The clause states the intended result: people will select him (you) out from the rebels.
segments.s411-g9: بداند is a 3rd-person jussive/optative form (let him know / may he know) of دانستن; که introduces the content clause. هستی تو is a nominal phrase meaning 'your being / that you are' with the pronoun تو postposed for emphasis (typical Persian order: predicate + pronoun). خسرونژاد is an appositive adjective phrase 'of Khosrow's descent / scion of Khosrow' that completes the content of the clause.
segments.s411-g10: کند is a 3rd-person subjunctive/capricious form of کردن used here to express a wish or make something happen ('may/make ...'). آفریننده را uses را as the definite-object/topic marker attached to 'the Creator'. بر تو is a prepositional phrase 'upon you' and یاد is a noun 'remembrance' used in a causative structure with کند: literally 'may (he) make the Creator's remembrance be upon you' = 'may the Creator remember you/may His remembrance rest on you.'
segments.s411-g11: ببر is the imperative 'take!' (command form of بردن). بالای is a prepositional/relational element 'on/above' (here linking the numeral to the following descriptive phrase). زرین is the adjective 'golden' qualifying ستام; ستام (from ستاد/ستا) functions as a noun 'praise/tribute' ('golden tribute' / 'golden praise') — the phrase 'پنج بالای زرین ستام' thus instructs to take 'five golden [praises/tributes]'.
segments.s411-g12: This clause contains an elided or irregular imperative order: (ده) 'give' is implied in the sequence. سرافراز is an adjective 'honored / lofty' qualifying موبد ('mōbed, Zoroastrian priest'), and نیک‌نام is a further epithet 'well‑named / reputable'. Grammatically the sequence commands giving/presenting an honored, well‑reputed mobed (i.e., present him with a distinguished priest).
segments.s411-g13: هم is a particle 'also/even' adding inclusiveness. از راه تا introduces a spatial span 'from the road up to / as far as'; خان رستم can mean 'Rostam's khan/inn (or house)' and is the endpoint specified by تا. بران is an adverbial/locative particle here meaning 'thither / thereon' (classical/poetic usage), so the whole segment directs movement 'also go from the road as far as Rostam's khan.'
segments.s411-g14: مکن is the prohibitive imperative 'do not'. کار is 'work / task' and بر خویشتن is a prepositional phrase 'upon yourself' (بر + reflexive خویشتن). The final بر گران literally 'too heavy' or 'heavy upon' — together: 'Do not make the work heavy upon yourself' = 'do not burden yourself with heavy work.' The double بر expresses the relation and the adjectival force in poetic style.
segments.s411-g15: درودش is درود 'greeting/blessing' with the enclitic pronoun -ش 'to him' (object/recipient). ده (imperative 'give') appears in the segment text but the word list focuses on in-situ words; the structure درودش ده از ما = 'give him our greeting(s)'. و links the next clause: خوبی نمای is an imperative 'show kindness / do good' where نمای is the verbal form of 'to show' (نمود/نما).
segments.s411-g16: بیارای here is an instruction to 'array/ornament/adorn' (poetic imperative feel) applied to گفتار 'speech' — literally 'adorn (your) speech'. و connects چربی فزای: چربی 'fat' (literal) and فزای is a jussive/imperative-form 'increase' (from فزودن); together 'increase the fat' meaning 'augment the provisions (literally add more fat/food)' — a conventional phrase asking that material reward be increased.
segments.s411-g49: سوی او یکی نامه ننوشته‌ای is an independent clause: سوی او (toward him/to him) + یکی نامه (a single letter) + ننوشته‌ای. ننوشته‌ای = ن (negative) + نوشته‌ای (perfect/participial form with 2nd-person singular ending) so the whole verb means “you have not written.” Word order places the prepositional phrase سوی او up front and the negative perfect verb at the end, as is normal in Persian poetry.
segments.s411-g50: از آرایش بندگی گشته‌ای is a second clause describing a resulting state: از + [آرایش بندگی] (literally “from/of the adornment/array of servitude”) + گشته‌ای (perfect of گشتن with 2nd-person singular ending, “you have become/turned”). The negative from the previous clause is not repeated; this clause states a present state formed by the perfective 'گشته‌ای' meaning 'you have become/assumed.'
segments.s411-g51: نرفتی به درگاه او بنده‌وار: نرفتی is the simple past/negative 2nd-person singular (“you did not go”), with the negating n- prefix; به درگاه او is the prepositional phrase “to his court/presence”; بنده‌وار is an adverbial adjective meaning “servilely/like a servant.” The verb comes at the beginning of the segment (poetic inversion) and the manner adverb follows the prepositional object.
segments.s411-g52: نخواهی به گیتی کسی شهریار uses نخواهی as a negative future/auxiliary-like verb with an omitted subject (you): here it functions “you will not…”; به گیتی = “in/throughout the world”; کسی in the scope of the negation means “anyone” (i.e. not king over anyone); شهریار is the predicative noun “ruler/king.” Word order places the negative auxiliary before the circumstantial phrases and the predicate at the end.
segments.s411-g53: ز هوشنگ و جم و فریدون گرد: ز is the poetic preposition 'from' or 'since'; a list of ancestral/royal names follows. گرد here follows the names and functions like an adverb/collective particle meaning “around/throughout/the circle of” or “from the line of,” marking the span or circle associated with those names. This phrase sets the temporal/lineage frame for the following clause.
segments.s411-g54: که از تخم ضحاک شاهی ببرد: که introduces the subordinate/result clause (“that/so that”); از تخم ضحاک = 'from the seed/offspring of Zahhak'; شاهی is 'kingship' (object); ببرد is the present/imperfective 3rd-person form of بردن with پیش‌وندِ ب- used in narrative/subjunctive sense — here best read as 'would be taken' / 'would be taken away' (i.e. kingship taken from Zahhak’s line). The verb is active in form but the sense is that kingship is removed from that seed.
segments.s411-g55: همی رو چنین تا سر کیقباد: همی + رو is a poetic continuous/instructional phrase meaning 'go on/continue thus' (همی = continually/still; رو = 'go' / 'proceed' in an older poetic imperative/present sense). چنین = 'thus'; تا introduces a limit 'until'; سر کیقباد = 'the (reign/time/occasion) of Kay Qobad' (سر here = head/top/reign). The whole segment gives a temporal boundary for the action.
segments.s411-g56: که تاج فریدون به سر بر نهاد: که = 'when/that' introducing the subordinate clause; تاج فریدون = 'Fereydun's crown' (or 'the crown of Fereydun'); به سر = 'onto the head'; بر نهاد is the past tense of نهادن (نهاد = 'placed/put'), with بر ('on/upon') preceding it — standard construction 'تاج به سر نهاد' = 'placed the crown on (his) head.'
segments.s411-g81: نرفتی is the past tense, 2nd-person singular with the negative prefix ن- (you did not go). The following ب + دان (here written separately) corresponds to classical بدَان = به + آن 'to/that', introducing the prepositional phrase naming the place (نامور بارگاه = 'renowned court'). Word order places the negated verb before the destination phrase for emphasis.
segments.s411-g82: نکردی is another past, 2nd-person singular with negation (you did not do / you did not give). Here it governs the action 'to cast/do' (i.e., give a look). Again ب + دان functions as بدَان 'to that', and نامداران نگاه is the object phrase 'a look to the illustrious ones' (نگاه = gaze). The sequence verb — prepositional phrase is a common classical Persian ordering for emphasis.
segments.s411-g83: کرانی here is a noun 'boundaries/limits' (object/theme). گرفتستی is a past tense, 2nd-person singular perfect ('you seized / you took possession of'). اندر جهان is a prepositional phrase 'in the world' (اندر = in/within). Classical Persian often places the object before the verb and the locative after it.
segments.s411-g84: که introduces a subordinate clause ('that' / 'so that' / 'whereas') giving additional comment. داری here is the present auxiliary/lexical verb meaning 'you hold/you keep' (progressive sense, 'are keeping'); همی is an adverb 'always/still' emphasizing continuity; خویشتن را uses the emphatic/reflexive noun with را marking it as the definite object; نهان is the predicate adjective 'hidden'. The clause means 'that you continually keep yourself hidden.'
segments.s411-g85: فرامش is the noun 'forgetfulness' used elliptically as the action 'making forgotten'; ترا is the object clitic combination (ت = 2nd-person object + را = object marker) meaning 'you'. مهتران (the great/elder ones/chiefs) is the agent. چون here functions as 'how' in a rhetorical question, and کنند is the present/subjunctive/optative form 'they do/make' (used to pose the question 'how could they make you forgotten?').
segments.s411-g86: مگر introduces the exception/condition 'unless'. مغز و دل‌پاک are the direct objects 'brain and pure heart' (دل‌پاک = 'pure-hearted / pure heart'). بیرون کند(ند) is the verb phrase 'to pull out / take out' (بیرون = out; کنند = they do), so the clause reads 'unless they pull out the brain and the pure heart.' The clause gives the violent exception that would make forgetting possible.
segments.s411-g87: همیشه is an adverb 'always/ever'. همه modifies نیکویی and means 'all / entire' (here 'every/all goodness'); نیکویی is the noun 'goodness/virtue'. خواستی is the past/present-poetic 2nd-person form 'you wished/desired' (classical usage; here simple past/ perfect sense).
segments.s411-g88: به فرمان شاهان is a prepositional phrase 'by the command of kings' (به = by/at, فرمان = command, شاهان = kings). بیاراستی is an archaic/poetic form related to آراستن (to adorn/arrange) with a prefixed form; here it functions as a past 2nd-person singular 'you adorned/you set in order' — i.e., you put goodness in order at the kings' command. The verb is somewhat archaic/poetic in form.
segments.s411-g121: This is a simple nominative list of people/agents. The sequence Zواره فرامرز و دستان سام lists proper names and a noun phrase: زواره (a personal name), فرامرز (another name), و (the conjunction 'and'), دستان here means 'the (mighty) hands/men' (i.e., the followers/men) and سام is the proper name (Sam) to which those men belong. Word order is basic enumerative: names/phrases separated by و.
segments.s411-g122: This is a noun phrase in apposition: جهاندیده is an attributive adjective meaning 'world‑experienced' or 'worldly‑wise' modifying رودابه (the proper name Rudabeh), and نیک‌نام is a postpositive adjective meaning 'well‑reputed' or 'of good name'. The three words together describe Rudabeh's character/status.
segments.s411-g123: همه is a vocative/imperative address 'everyone' or 'all'; پند من is the direct object 'my advice/counsel' (پند = advice, من = my), and یک به یک ('one by one') is an adverbial phrase. بشنوید is the second‑person plural imperative or exhortative 'hear/listen'. The clause therefore commands the assembled to listen to the speaker's advice in order.
segments.s411-g124: بدین = 'by/with this' (literally 'with this/thus'); خوب‌گفتار من is 'my good words' or 'my eloquent counsel' (خوب‌گفتار attributive to 'من' here meaning 'my well‑spoken words'); بگروید is second‑person plural imperative 'adopt/accept' (literally 'take on' or 'bend to' — here 'accept my counsel'). Together: 'Accept my good words/this counsel.' Note: the tokenizer split بدین into ب + دین; دین as listed senses does not match the actual 'این' sense, so that token is treated as part of the idiom 'بدین'.
segments.s411-g125: نباید is an impersonal modal 'must not/should not'; که introduces the subordinate clause 'that ...'; این خانه ('this house') is the subject/topic and ویران is a predicate adjective 'ruined/destroyed' placed before the verb; شود is the subjunctive/optative/impersonal present of شدن 'become/be ruined'. The structure expresses a prohibition: 'It must not be that this house be ruined.'
segments.s411-g126: به کام = 'to the desire/satisfaction of'; دلیران ایران is a genitive/group: 'the brave ones of Iran' (دلیران = brave men, ایران here denotes the people/realm); شود repeats the subjunctive 'become'. The phrase qualifies the prohibition: the house must not be destroyed so that it comes to satisfy the wishes of Iran's warriors.
segments.s411-g127: چو = 'when' (temporal conjunction). بسته ترا is a participial construction: بسته 'bound' modifies the object ترا ('you'); را is the definite object marker attached to ترا (here shown as ت + را). نزد شاه آورم = 'I bring (you) into the presence of the king' (نزد = 'before/in the presence of', شاه = 'king', آورم = 1st‑person present/subjunctive 'I bring' or 'I will bring'). The clause means 'When I bring you bound before the king...'.
segments.s411-g128: بدو = 'to him' (i.e., 'before him' or 'to that man'); بر here has the sense 'against' (bringing something 'against' someone); فراوان qualifying گناه = 'many/abundant faults/charges'; آورم = 'I will bring' (1st person). The clause says the speaker will present many charges against the addressee before the king.
segments.s411-g73: ز is the poetic preposition 'from' introducing the extent (زِ تورانِ زمین = 'from the land of Turan'). زمین here means 'land/country' (not the physical ground). تا marks the limit 'as far as', and در هند و روم specifies the places 'in India and Rome' (در = 'in'). The phrase gives the geographic span: from Turan to India and Rome.
segments.s411-g74: جهان شد is a simple past/stative: 'the world became'. مر is an archaic enclitic meaning 'to me' (literary 'for me'). او را uses را as the definite/direct-object/topic marker attached to the pronoun 'he/his', functioning here as 'to him'/'his' in the sense 'the world (came) to him'. چو introduces a simile 'like/as', and یک مهره موم = 'a single bead of wax' (مهره = bead, موم = wax). Word order is literary: the indirect/possessive relation is expressed by placing او + را after which مر ('to me') shows the speaker's viewpoint.
segments.s411-g75: ز دشت introduces source/location 'from the plain'. سواران = 'horsemen/riders'. نیزه گزار is a compound meaning 'spear-bearers' (one who carries a spear); the provided lemma senses do not list this verbal-agent sense for گزار, so that element is being used derivationally here as 'bearer/carrier' rather than the listed senses.
segments.s411-g76: به درگاه = 'to/at the court' (درگاه = royal court/threshold). اویند is an archaic/formal plural verbal form here meaning roughly 'they are/attend' (the supplied senses 'they hang/they fasten' do not capture the intended copulative/existential nuance in context, so sense is uncertain). چندی سوار = 'several/some riders' (چندی = a few/several); the phrase places the quantifier after the verb cluster in a poetic order.
segments.s411-g77: فرستندش is the past plural 'they sent' with the enclitic object -ش = 'him/it'. از مرزها = 'from the borders/frontiers'. باژ و ساو are two items presented in apposition; باژ is best read as a cry/noise here, while ساو may be a place-name or proper name (both readings occur in classical usage), so their precise referent is ambiguous but they are listed together as what was sent/from where.
segments.s411-g78: که introduces an explanatory/relative clause 'for/that'. با جنگ او = 'with his war' or 'by means of his warfare' (با = with/by, جنگ = war, او = his). نیستشان is a fused negative meaning 'is not to them / they do not have' (literally 'is not for them'), and زور و تاو are two coordinated nouns 'strength (زور) and radiance/glory (تاو)'. Together: 'they have neither strength nor radiance (in the face of his war)'.
segments.s411-g79: ازان = 'thereupon / because of that'. گفتم normally means 'I said', but the provided lemma senses list conversational meanings rather than the simple first-person past 'I said' — the intended function here is the speaker's report 'I said'. این با توای پهلوان is addressed to the hero: این = 'this', با = 'to/for', توای (from توا) is an archaic/poetic vocative/adjective 'thou art able / capable', and پهلوان = 'champion/hero'. The phrase overall is 'I said this to you, O able hero' (word order is literary/poetic).
segments.s411-g80: که introduces the content clause 'that'. او = 'he'. از تو آزرده = 'distressed/annoyed by you' (از indicates the cause/source of distress). دارد is the present auxiliary 'has' or 'is bearing' and روان here means 'soul/spirit' (روان دارد = 'his spirit is troubled' / 'he is grieved'). Together: 'that he is distressed by you' (i.e., he is troubled because of you).
segments.s411-g97: برآشفت is a past simple verb (preterite) meaning ‘became angry’ placed before its temporal phrase (typical Classical Persian order). یک روز is the temporal adjunct ‘one day’. و links the clauses, and سوگند خورد is a coordinated clause: سوگند ‘oath’ + خورد, here the past of خوردن used idiomatically with سوگند to mean ‘swore (an oath)’.
segments.s411-g98: به‌روز … و شب … is a prepositional phrase used to mark the objects of the oath (‘by the day … and the night …’). سپید and لاژورد are adjectives describing روز and شب; the adjective follows the noun (poetic word order). لاژورد is a poetic/loan word for lapis/azure.
segments.s411-g99: که introduces a subordinate content clause. او را is the definite object (object marker را follows the pronoun). به جز is the compound meaning ‘except’, followed by بسته ‘bound/fastened’ describing the exceptional condition; در بارگاه is the locative ‘in the court’, placed after the exception.
segments.s411-g100: نبیند is the negative present/subjunctive form (classical usage giving a future/optative sense here) meaning ‘will not/shall not see’. ازین پس is the temporal phrase ‘from now on’ (از + این/ین + پس). جهاندار شاه is the title ‘the sovereign king’, with the epithet following the temporal phrase as subject.
segments.s411-g101: کنون ‘now’ is an adverb at the clause start; من is the explicit subject. ز ایران is the source (‘from Iran’). بدین is the compound prepositional/adverbial ‘to this / to here’ (here shown segmented as ب + دین in the data but functioning together), and آمدم is 1st person past ‘I came’.
segments.s411-g102: نبد is an archaic negative past/copula meaning ‘there was not’ or ‘did not exist’. شاه دستور is a genitival/possessive construction ‘the king’s order/command’. تا is temporal ‘until’, and دم زدم is an idiomatic phrase (‘I made a sound / I spoke’) where دم ‘breath/moment’ plus زدم (past of زدن) conveys uttering or making noise.
segments.s411-g103: بپرهیز is an imperative meaning ‘beware/avoid’. و links a second imperative phrase پیچان شو — پیـچان here functions as ‘evasive/crafty’ or ‘twisting’ and شو is the imperative ‘become’ (be evasive). از introduces the source/agent of avoidance ‘from his anger’ with اوی the archaic possessive ‘his’.
segments.s411-g104: ندیدی is a rhetorical negative perfect/past (‘have you not seen’ / ‘you did not see’). که introduces the subordinate clause. خشم آورد is a simple past construction where آورد (from آوردن) means ‘brought/cause/produced’, and چشم اوی ‘his eye’ (اوی = his) is the object: the clause says that his anger produced/made his eye (i.e. produced a glaring look).
segments.s411-g89: اگر introduces a hypothetical conditional ('if'). بر + شمارد is the classical separable/preverbal form of شمردن ('to count up/ reckon') here in 3rd‑person singular, used as the protasis verb ('were to count'). کسی is the (indefinite) subject following the verb (word order typical of older Persian), and رنجِ تو is the direct object 'your suffering'.
segments.s411-g90: به گیتی is a locative/goal phrase 'in/throughout the world' placed before the predicate. فزون آید is a predicate in which فزون (more/greater) is predicative and آید ('comes/occurs') supplies the verbal sense — together 'would be greater'. از introduces the standard Persian comparative 'than', linking گنجِ تو ('your treasure/wealth') as the standard of comparison. The tense/aspect matches the conditional sense of the previous clause.
segments.s411-g91: ز شاهان ('from/of the kings') marks the group being spoken about; کسی here functions as the indefinite subject. بر چنین داستان is a prepositional phrase 'about/on such a tale/matter' (بر = 'about/on', چنین = 'such', داستان = 'tale/story'). Word order places the group phrase and indefinite before the comment that follows in the next segment.
segments.s411-g92: ز بنده here sets up the comparison/association 'with the servant' (z = 'with/from' in classical usage). نبودند is the 3rd‑person plural past negative 'were not'. همداستان is a predicate adjective/nominal meaning 'of the same mind' or 'in agreement', so the whole clause says the kings were not in agreement with the servant.
segments.s411-g93: مرا (here split as م + را in the manuscript) is the object clitic 'me' marked by را; گفت is the past verb 'said/told' (3rd person), and رستم is the subject placed after the verb (old word order). ز بس خواسته is an ablative/circumstantial phrase 'from/after much asking' where ز = 'from/because of', بس = 'so much/enough', and خواسته is a verbal adjective/participle ('having asked' / 'demanded').
segments.s411-g94: هم = 'also/likewise' introduces the added result. از کشور و گنج = 'of/from the country (realm) and the treasure(wealth)' lists the things affected, and آراسته is a past participle/adjective 'adorned, furnished' describing that they have been made splendid — together meaning 'he had also adorned the realm and its wealth.'
segments.s411-g95: به زاول is an adverbial phrase 'from the outset / at the very beginning' (به = classical preposition, زاول = 'the outset'). نشستست and گشتست are older/perfective verbal forms (verb + enclitic -ست), respectively 'he sat (down)' and 'he became/turned', and مست is a predicative adjective 'drunk/intoxicated' — together: 'he sat from the outset and became drunk.'
segments.s411-g96: نگیرد is a 3rd‑person singular negative form of گرفتن ('does/ would not take, does not seize') used here as a general statement. کس with the negative verb yields 'no one' (negated indefinite). از مست = 'from the drunk (person)'; چیزی = 'anything/something' (object); به دست is an adverbial phrase 'into hand / into one's possession'. The clause states a general truth: no one takes anything from a drunk by hand.
segments.s411-g113: پشوتن is a proper name (the subject). برین is an adjectival/superlative form (literally built from بر + -ین) meaning 'foremost' or 'chief'. بر here introduces the domain ('in' / 'with respect to') and گوای (from گوا) functions as 'word, testimony, counsel' — the clause ends with the archaic predicative form منست (copular/qualifying), so the whole segment says 'Peshotan is foremost in counsel/testimony.'
segments.s411-g114: روان و خرد is a compound subject 'spirit and wisdom'. رهنمای is a predicate noun 'guide' and منست is the same archaic copula/predicative as above; word order is Subject + Predicate (noun + copula), giving 'spirit and wisdom are (my) guide(s)'.
segments.s411-g115: همی is an adverbial particle marking continuity/emphasis ('long/continually' or 'indeed'); جستم is 1st‑person past 'I sought' (here 'seek' sense). The prepositional phrase از تو 'from you' marks the source, من is an explicit topical subject 'I' for emphasis, آرام is the object 'peace/rest', and شاه is a vocative 'O king' appended at the end.
segments.s411-g116: ولیکن is a concessive 'but/yet'. همی again adds emphasis. از تو 'from you' is the source phrase; دیدم is the 1st‑person past 'I saw/perceived'; گناه is the object meaning 'fault/blame' (here 'fault' fits the context). The clause means 'but I perceived fault in you.'
segments.s411-g117: پدر شهریارست is a nominal clause: پدر 'father' + شهریارست (predicate) meaning 'is king' (an adjectival/predicative form). و links the clauses. من کهترم places the speaker ('I') with کهترم ('I am lesser/inferior' — -م is the 1st‑person ending).
segments.s411-g118: ز فرمان او is a prepositional phrase 'from his command' (ز = poetic 'from/of'); یک زمان 'one moment' (a single time). نگذرم is 1st‑person present/negative form 'I will not pass/depart' (here idiomatically 'I will not disobey or depart from'). Word order is 'From his command one moment I will not depart.'
segments.s411-g119: همه is the universal subject 'everyone/everything'. دوده is a lexical item here following 'همه' (its specific referent is context-dependent). اکنون 'now' is the temporal adverb. بباید is the modal 'must/ought' and نشست is used as a noun 'a sitting/session/meeting' (so the clause means 'Everyone must now (have) a sitting/meeting').
segments.s411-g120: This clause strings verbal‑noun constructions: زدن رای ('to put forth/strike an opinion' — زدَن + رای) and سودن ('to profit/avail') followed by بدین کار دست (بدین = به + این 'to/for this', کار 'work/affair', دست 'hand' used idiomatically as 'to take in hand'). Grammatically, infinitival/masdar verbs (زدن, سودن) govern the nouns that follow and express purpose: give counsel and make this work profitable by taking it up.
segments.s411-g105: چو is a subordinating conjunction meaning 'when/if'. اینجا (here) precedes the verb. بیایی and کنی are second‑person present‑subjunctive forms (verb stem + ـیـ) used in a conditional/subordinate clause; the two verbs are linked by و. فرمان کنی is a noun + کردن construction ('to give a command'), with کنی serving as the verbalizing auxiliary.
segments.s411-g106: روان را marks 'the soul' as a definite direct object (را). به introduces the purpose/relation 'to/for' (here: 'as/for apology'), پوزش is the noun 'apology/pardon', and گروگان کنی is the verb phrase 'you make (it) a hostage' where کنی is again the present‑subjunctive/auxiliary 'to make'.
segments.s411-g107: به here functions as an oath/prepositional particle 'by'. خورشید رخشان is 'the radiant sun' with the adjective رخشان following the noun. The phrase is coordinated with و plus جان زریر, where جان (life/soul) is specified by the proper name زریر.
segments.s411-g108: به again 'by' for an oath. جان پدرم = 'the life/soul of my father' where پدر + ـم is the possessive 'my' (here shown as separate elements). آن جهاندار شیر is an emphatic appositive: آن 'that/the', جهاندار 'sovereign/conqueror', شیر 'lion' — a descriptive title following the oath.
segments.s411-g109: که introduces a subordinate/clausal purpose 'that/so that'. من is the subject 'I'. زین here functions archaically as 'from this/for this' (classical زین ≈ از این) — not one of the provided senses. پشیمان کنم is an adjective + کردن construction meaning 'I make (someone) repent' with کنَم (first person subjunctive/optative) and شاه را is the definite object marked by را (the king is the one who will be made regretful).
segments.s411-g110: برافرزوم is an optative/jussive first‑person form meaning 'may I create' or 'I will create' (a wish/resolve). این اختر و ماه را is the object phrase 'this star and moon' with the demonstrative این and the definitive marker را; the verb precedes its object here, a common poetic order.
segments.s411-g111: که introduces the clause. من is 'I'. زین again functions archaically ('from/for this' ≈ ازین) and does not match the listed senses. the second که (که گفتم) is a relative/subordinate 'which I said' with گفتم 'I said' (past tense) — the provided senses for گفتم do not include 'said' so none fit. نجویم is the present subjunctive with negative/optative sense 'I will not seek' or 'may I not seek' (here: a resolution), and فروغ is the object 'radiance/glory'.
segments.s411-g112: نگردم is the verb with the negative prefix ن‑ plus گردم ('I turn/go about'), giving 'I will not turn/go about' — a firm refusal. به هر کار marks 'in/to any affair/work' (به + هر + کار). گرد here functions as 'about/around' or as part of the verbal sense 'to go about', linking to دروغ 'falsehood/lie' to yield 'in any business of falsehood' or 'to no deceitful deed'.

sentences.s411-s29: When Gushtasp was king, there was not a single renowned (man) in battle, in revelry, in counsel, or in the hunt.
sentences.s411-s30: He embraced the pure Good Religion; error and waywardness were hidden.
sentences.s411-s31: When the sun became the world's guide and sovereign, corrupt teaching and the demon's way were hidden.
sentences.s411-s32: After that, when Arjasp came to battle, his host was like leopards and its chief like a whale.
sentences.s411-s21: that you acquired many honours, treasure, and an army — valuable horses, a throne, and a crown/helmet.
sentences.s411-s22: You obtained (them) from our ancestors when you hurried swiftly into servitude.
sentences.s411-s23: What wealth Lohrasp the king had in the world — you did not go to that palace.
sentences.s411-s24: When he gave the city of Iran to Gushtasp, not a trace of that throne came to you.
sentences.s411-s9: Tell him that whoever becomes a lofty world‑ruler will be unharmed by every evil.
sentences.s411-s10: One must give thanks to the Creator, for he is the eternal knower of goodness.
sentences.s411-s11: When goodness increases, by restraint he keeps his head away from ill‑temper.
sentences.s411-s12: It increases for him success and treasure; he was joyful in the house of Sepanj.
sentences.s411-s65: Let us go before him and entreat him at his feet, that from anger and hatred he may again restore (him) to his place in peace.
sentences.s411-s66: I will not remain (stay) so that a breeze should blow upon you in such a way that it would befit my very being.
sentences.s411-s17: As you do to others, so shall you be treated; whatever words you speak, so shall you hear.
sentences.s411-s18: Now we shall measure you by a standard; it is right not to add to this nor to diminish it.
sentences.s411-s19: For you passed countless years, and in the world you saw many a king.
sentences.s411-s20: If you question by the path of reason, you will know that such a thing does not belong here.
sentences.s411-s33: No one knew the number of his host; the famed king received him.
sentences.s411-s34: He made a cemetery on the plain of vengeance, so that no wide surface of the earth was visible.
sentences.s411-s35: Truly, this tale will not grow old among the great until the Resurrection.
sentences.s411-s36: Now, from his east straight to the west, he continually breaks the backs of male lions.
sentences.s411-s13: When one keeps away from evil deeds, one finds paradise in that world.
sentences.s411-s14: Good and bad alike come upon us; whoever has wisdom knows this.
sentences.s411-s15: At last the bed was the dark earth; the soul takes flight toward the pure God.
sentences.s411-s16: In the world, whoever knows goodness should strive and make (arrangements) with the rulers.
sentences.s411-s1: He ordered that Bahman come before him, and he gave him admonitions beyond measure.
sentences.s411-s2: He said to him, 'Mount the black steed; deck your body in Chinese brocade.'
sentences.s411-s3: Put on your head the kingly helmet, its ornamentation all the Pahlavi jewels.
sentences.s411-s4: So that whoever sees you will single you out from among the rebels.
sentences.s411-s5: Let him know that you are of Khosrow's line; may the Creator's remembrance be upon you.
sentences.s411-s6: Take five golden praises/tributes, and give (him) an honored, well‑named mobed.
sentences.s411-s7: Also, from the road as far as Rostam's khan go thither; do not lay a heavy burden of work upon yourself.
sentences.s411-s8: Give him our greetings and show goodwill; adorn your speech and increase the fat (i.e., augment provisions).
sentences.s411-s25: You have not written a single letter to him; you have assumed the garb of servitude.
sentences.s411-s26: You did not go to his court in a servile way; you will not be anyone's sovereign in the world.
sentences.s411-s27: From the (time/line) of Hushang and Jam and Fereydun — so that the kingship would be taken from Zahhak’s seed.
sentences.s411-s28: Continue thus until the time of Kay Qobad, when Fereydun placed the crown upon his head.
sentences.s411-s41: You did not go to that renowned court; you did not cast a look upon those famed men.
sentences.s411-s42: You have taken possession of the world's boundaries, yet you keep yourself hidden.
sentences.s411-s43: How will the chiefs make you forgotten, unless they pull out your brain and pure heart?
sentences.s411-s44: You have always desired every goodness; by the command of kings you set it in order / adorned it.
sentences.s411-s61: Zavareh, Faramarz, and the men of Sam; the worldly‑wise Rudabeh, well‑reputed.
sentences.s411-s62: All, hear my counsel one by one; accept my good words.
sentences.s411-s63: This house must not be ruined, nor should it become the satisfaction of Iran's brave (men).
sentences.s411-s64: When I bring you bound before the king, I will bring many charges against you to him.
sentences.s411-s37: From the land of Turan as far as India and Rome, the world, in my view, became his like a single bead of wax.
sentences.s411-s38: From the plain come the horsemen — the spear-bearers; a few riders are at his court.
sentences.s411-s39: They sent him from the frontiers — 'bâzh' and 'Sâv' — for with his war they have neither strength nor radiance.
sentences.s411-s40: Because of that I said: 'This one is for you, O capable champion, for he is distressed by you.'
sentences.s411-s49: One day he became angry and swore by the white day and the azure night.
sentences.s411-s50: That the sovereign king would no longer see him in the court from now on, except when he is bound.
sentences.s411-s51: Now I have come from Iran to this place; there was no royal order until I made a sound (spoke).
sentences.s411-s52: Beware and be evasive of his anger; have you not seen that his anger brought forth his eye?
sentences.s411-s45: If anyone were to count your suffering, it would be greater in the world than your wealth.
sentences.s411-s46: Among the kings there was no one who, about such a matter, was of the same mind as the servant.
sentences.s411-s47: Rostam told me that, having demanded so much, he had likewise adorned both the realm and its treasury.
sentences.s411-s48: From the very beginning he sat down and became intoxicated; no one takes anything from a drunk into his hand.
sentences.s411-s57: Peshotan is foremost in counsel; spirit and wisdom are my guides.
sentences.s411-s58: I have long sought peace from you, O king; yet from you I perceived fault.
sentences.s411-s59: His father is king, and I am the younger/inferior one; from his command I will not depart for a single moment.
sentences.s411-s60: Now everyone must sit (hold a council); to set forth counsel and to make this enterprise profitable by putting one's hand to the work.
sentences.s411-s53: If/when you come here and give a command, you make the soul a hostage to apology.
sentences.s411-s54: By the radiant sun and the soul of Zarir; by my father's life — that sovereign lion.
sentences.s411-s55: That I may make the king regret this; may I create this star and moon.
sentences.s411-s56: That I, because of what I said, will not seek glory; I will not turn to any deed of falsehood.
